title: Whipped shea body butter
subtitle: A light aerated body moisturiser with shea butter and jojoba
excerpt: Shea butter whipped to a soft, airy texture with jojoba oil and a small amount of arrowroot powder to reduce the greasy residue. No water, no emulsifier, no preservative needed. Fills approximately three 80 ml jars. Lavender essential oil is optional.
sourceNotes: Zero Waste Nerd whipped shea butter tutorial (zerowastenerd.com, accessed May 2026): arrowroot ratio for reducing drag. Essential Oil Safety (Tisserand and Young, 2nd ed.): lavender at body-lotion dilution.

recipe.yieldDescription: ~250 g whipped butter (3 x 80 ml jars)
recipe.batchNotes: Double the batch easily; use a stand mixer for larger quantities.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The whipped butter firms up overnight. Make a day ahead if gifting so the texture is fully set.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months in a cool place. Shea butter goes rancid faster in heat. If the butter smells sharp or waxy-rancid, discard. Keep water out of the jar; wet fingers will reduce the shelf life.

glossaryTerms.definition: Shea butter whips best when partially solid but still pliable, around 18 to 22 degrees Celsius. If the shea is fully liquid (above 37 degrees Celsius) it will not aerate. Chill it in the fridge until the surface is solid but the interior yields to a finger press, then beat immediately.
Arrowroot powder absorbs a small amount of surface oil after application, which reduces the greasy or tacky feel on skin without changing the moisturising properties of the butter or carrier oil.

content.text: Whipping shea butter incorporates air into the fat structure, changing the dense, waxy block into a light mousse-like texture that spreads more easily and absorbs faster. The
matters: too warm and the butter stays liquid; too cold and it will not beat smoothly. A brief chill in the fridge followed by immediate whipping gives the best result.

powder reduces the oily drag that uncut shea leaves on skin. Lavender essential oil is optional; leave it out for an unscented version. Patch test on the inner forearm before applying to the full body.

items.prepNote: Unrefined shea butter. Refined shea is odourless and smoother but has fewer natural fatty acids.
Lightens the final texture and adds stability. Add before whipping.
Cosmetic-grade arrowroot. Sift it in after whipping to avoid lumps.
2% of total batch weight. Lavandula angustifolia. Leave out for an unscented version. Add after the arrowroot is incorporated.

content.text: If the shea butter is very soft or has been in a warm room, transfer it to the mixing bowl and refrigerate for 20 to 30 minutes until the surface is firm but the centre still yields slightly when pressed. It should not be hard all the way through.

Add the jojoba oil to the chilled shea butter. Beat with the hand mixer on medium speed for 3 to 4 minutes, increasing to high for the last minute. The butter will turn pale and fluffy, roughly doubling in volume.
Fold in arrowroot and optional scent

Sift the arrowroot powder into the whipped butter and fold through gently with a spatula. If using lavender essential oil, add it now and mix briefly with the hand mixer on low. Spoon into clean jars, smooth the surface, and label with the making date.

Store in a cool place below 20 degrees Celsius. Warm rooms above 25 degrees Celsius will soften the butter until it loses its whipped texture; refrigerate in summer. Use dry fingers or a spatula to scoop from the jar. Shelf life is 12 months. Discard if the butter develops a sharp or waxy-rancid smell.
